p:
Which of the following modified inequalities are satisfied for all real numbers $a, b, c, x, y, z$ which satisfy the conditions $x \leq a$, $y \leq b$, and $z \leq c$?
$\text{I}. \ xy + yz + zx \leq ab + bc + ca + 3$
$\text{II}. \ x^2 + y^2 + z^2 \leq a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 3$
$\text{III}. \ xyz \leq abc + 1$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{None are satisfied} \qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ \text{I only} \qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ \text{II only} \qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ \text{III only} \qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ \text{All are satisfied}$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{None are satisfied} \qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ \text{I only} \qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ \text{II only} \qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ \text{III only} \qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ \text{All are satisfied}$
ch:
\text{All are satisfied}$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{None are satisfied} \qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ \text{I only} \qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ \text{II only} \qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ \text{III only} \qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ \text{All are satisfied}

p:
A unique positive integer $ n $ satisfies the equation:
$$
\log_4(\log_8 n) = \log_2(\log_2 n).
$$
Find the sum of the digits of $ n $.
A) 118
B) 127
C) 130
D) 144
E) 150

Which of the following statements is (are) equivalent to the statement "If a volcano on Mars erupts, then there will be no snowfall on Venus this year"?
$\textbf{(A) }\textbf{I. }\text{and }\textbf{II. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(B) }\textbf{III. }\text{and }\textbf{IV. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(C) }\textbf{II. }\text{and }\textbf{IV. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(D) }\textbf{II. }\text{and }\textbf{III. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(E) }\textbf{III. }\text{only}$

Calculate the average monthly sales from Booster's Club sales data. Sales from January to June are given as $150, 90, X, 180, 210, 240$ respectively, where $X$ represents the sales in March, which were 50% greater than those in February.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 162.5\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 165\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 167.5\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 170\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 172.5$

A scout troop purchases $1500$ candy bars at a rate of six for $3$. They sell all the candy bars at a rate of three for $2$. What is their profit, in dollars?
$\textbf{(A) } 150      \qquad \textbf{(B) }\ 200      \qquad \textbf{(C) }\ 250      \qquad \textbf{(D) }\ 300      \qquad \textbf{(E) }\ 350$

A basketball player made $ 7 $ baskets during a game, each basket worth either $ 2 $ or $ 3 $ points. How many different total point scores could the player achieve?
A. 5
B. 6
C. 7
D. 8
E. 9

The first three terms of an arithmetic progression are $x^2 - 3$, $x^2 + 3x + 2$, and $2x^2 + 5x + 6$. Find the value of $x$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{1 - \sqrt{5}}{2}\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ -1\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \text{both A and B}$

Eight times $A$'s money added to $B$'s money is more than $160.00$. Four times $A$'s money plus $B$'s money is $120.00$.
If $a$ represents $A$'s money in dollars and $b$ represents $B$'s money in dollars, then:
**A)** $a > 10$, $b > 80$  
**B)** $a > 10$, $b < 80$  
**C)** $a > 10$, $b = 80$  
**D)** $a > 10$, and as $a$ increases, $b$ decreases  
**E)** $2a = 3b$
a: \textbf{(D)}\ a > 10, and as a increases, b decreases

p:
Find the sum of all even digits in all numbers from 1 to 200. The function $ E(n) $ represents the sum of even digits in the number $ n $.
A) 600
B) 800
C) 1000
D) 1200

Determine which of the following statements is (are) equivalent to the statement "If a blue dragon on planet Gamma breathes fire, then the silver lion on planet Theta does not roar during storms".

$\textbf{(A) }\textbf{I. }\text{and }\textbf{II. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(B) }\textbf{III. }\text{and }\textbf{IV. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(C) }\textbf{II. }\text{and }\textbf{IV. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(D) }\textbf{II. }\text{and }\textbf{III. }\text{only}\qquad \textbf{(E) }\textbf{III. }\text{only}$
$\textbf{(A) }\textbf{I. }\text{and }\textbf{II. }\text{only}$
$\textbf{(B) }\textbf{III. }\text{and }\textbf{IV. }\text{only}$
$\textbf{(C) }\textbf{II. }\text{and }\textbf{IV. }\text{only}$
$\textbf{(D) }\textbf{II. }\text{and }\textbf{III. }\text{only}$
$\textbf{(E) }\textbf{III. }\text{only}$

In a newly designed cryptographic code, the first time a letter appears in a message, it is replaced by the letter that is 1 place to the right in the alphabet. Each subsequent time this letter appears, it is replaced by the letter that is the square of the position number of its appearance to the right. For instance, using this code, the word "finale" would encrypt to "gjobmf". What letter will replace the last 'i' in the message "Mini's visit is inspiring in Illinois, isn't it?"?
- **(A)** f
- **(B)** g
- **(C)** h
- **(D)** e
- **(E)** j

At Central High School, $\frac{3}{7}$ of the freshmen and $\frac{2}{3}$ of the sophomores took part in the Science Olympiad. Given that the number of freshmen and sophomore participants was the same, which of the following must be true?
- $\textbf{(A)}$ There are three times as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(B)}$ There are more than twice as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(C)}$ The number of freshmen equals the number of sophomores.
- $\textbf{(D)}$ There are $\frac{7}{3}$ times as many freshmen as sophomores.
- $\textbf{(E)}$ There are $\frac{14}{9}$ times as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(A)}$ There are three times as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(B)}$ There are more than twice as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(C)}$ The number of freshmen equals the number of sophomores.
- $\textbf{(D)}$ There are $\frac{7}{3}$ times as many freshmen as sophomores.
- $\textbf{(E)}$ There are $\frac{14}{9}$ times as many sophomores as freshmen.
ch:
$ There are three times as many sophomores as freshmen.
-

$ There are more than twice as many sophomores as freshmen.
-

$ The number of freshmen equals the number of sophomores.
-

$ There are $\frac{7}{3}$ times as many freshmen as sophomores.
-

$ There are $\frac{14}{9}$ times as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(A)}$ There are three times as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(B)}$ There are more than twice as many sophomores as freshmen.
- $\textbf{(C)}$ The number of freshmen equals the number of sophomores.
- $\textbf{(D)}$ There are $\frac{7}{3}$ times as many freshmen as sophomores.
- $\textbf{(E)}$ There are $\frac{14}{9}$ times as many sophomores as freshmen.

p:
Lucky Larry's teacher asked him to substitute numbers for $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, and $e$ in the expression $a+(b-(c+(d-e)))$ and evaluate the result. Larry ignored the parentheses but added and subtracted correctly and obtained the correct result by coincidence. The numbers Larry substituted for $a$, $b$, $c$, and $d$ were $5$, $3$, $4$, and $2$, respectively. What number did Larry substitute for $e$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 1 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 2 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 4$

The three-digit number $7a1$ is added to the number $184$ to give the three-digit number $b65$. If $b65$ is divisible by 11, then what is the value of $a + b$?
A) 6
B) 7
C) 8
D) 9
E) 10

In Rivertown Middle School, one third of the students take the bus, one fifth use carpool, and one eighth ride their scooters to school. The other students walk home. What fraction of students walk home?
A) $\frac{30}{120}$
B) $\frac{89}{120}$
C) $\frac{60}{120}$
D) $\frac{41}{120}$
E) $\frac{100}{120}$

Three boys have a collection of marbles. The number of marbles each boy has can be represented by $4x+2$, $3x-1$, and $5x+3$. The total number of marbles is 128. Find the number of marbles the second boy has.
A. 28
B. 30
C. 32
D. 34

Consider a modified pattern on line $\ell$ consisting of a regular, infinite series of alternating up-facing and down-facing triangles along with vertical and horizontal line segments connecting their bases. How many of the following four kinds of rigid motion transformations of the plane in which this figure is drawn, other than the identity transformation, will transform this figure into itself?

1. Some rotation around a point on line $\ell$
2. Some translation in the direction parallel to line $\ell$
3. The reflection across line $\ell$
4. Some reflection across a line perpendicular to line $\ell$
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0 \quad \textbf{(B)}\ 1 \quad \textbf{(C)}\ 2 \quad \textbf{(D)}\ 3 \quad \textbf{(E)}\ 4$

Suppose $a$ is $125\%$ of $b$. What percent of $a$ is $4b$?
$\textbf{(A) } 240\% \qquad \textbf{(B) } 280\% \qquad \textbf{(C) } 320\% \qquad \textbf{(D) } 350\% \qquad \textbf{(E) } 400\%$

Calculate the sum of the differences between the first hundred positive even integers and the preceding odd integers for those pairs where the even integer is divisible by 5.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 5 \qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ 10 \qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ 15 \qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ 20 \qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ 25$

The product of three consecutive positive integers is 6 times their sum. What is the sum of their squares?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 50 \qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ 71 \qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ 77 \qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ 92 \qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ 110$

A square playground of side length 2 meters is divided into four smaller congruent squares. An isosceles right triangle is constructed within each smaller square with its hypotenuse along one side of the square. If the sum of the areas of these four triangles equals the area of the square, find the length of one leg of one of the isosceles triangles.
A) $1 \text{ meter}$
B) $2 \text{ meters}$
C) $\sqrt{2} \text{ meters}$
D) $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \text{ meters}$

Car P traveled at a constant speed for a given time, represented by the dashed line in a graph. Car Q traveled at twice the speed of Car P, and Car R traveled at three times the speed of Car P for the same distance. If the speeds and travel times of Car Q and Car R are to be shown with solid lines, which type of graph would best illustrate the travel times and speeds of Car Q and Car R as compared to Car P?
A. Car Q's line is twice as high but the same length as Car P's. Car R's line is not shown.
B. Car Q's line is twice as high and half the length. Car R's line is three times as high and one-third the length.
C. Both Car Q and Car R maintain the same line length as Car P, but have different heights.
D. No solid lines are corresponding to Car Q and Car R, all lines are dashed.

Consider the following matrix:
$$
\left[\begin{matrix}5 & 10 & 0\\ 4 & 6 & 5\\ 6 & 4 & 5\end{matrix}\right]
$$
The three row sums and the three column sums of the array are currently not all the same. Determine the least number of entries that must be altered to make all six sums different from one another.
A) 1
B) 2
C) 3
D) 4
E) 5

For what value(s) of $k$ does the pair of equations $y = x^2$ and $y = 3x^2 + k$ have two identical solutions?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ -2\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 2\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 1\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ -1$

Four spheres of radius 2, each sit in one quadrant of the xy-plane, each being tangent to both x and y axes. What is the radius of the smallest sphere, centered at the origin, that completely contains these four spheres?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 2+\sqrt{2} \qquad \mathrm{(B)}\ \frac{\sqrt{2} + 2}{2} \qquad \mathrm{(C)}\ 2\sqrt{2} \qquad \mathrm{(D)}\ 2\sqrt{2} + 2 \qquad \mathrm{(E)}\ 4$

In $\triangle ABC$, where $AB=AC=10$ and $BC=12$, point $\overline{AH}$ is an altitude. Points $D$ and $E$ lie on sides $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{AB}$, respectively, such that $\overline{BD}$ and $\overline{CE}$ are angle bisectors, intersecting $\overline{AH}$ at $Q$ and $P$, respectively. Determine the length of segment $PQ$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0$  
$\textbf{(B)}\ 1$  
$\textbf{(C)}\ 2$  
$\textbf{(D)}\ 3$  
$\textbf{(E)}\ 4$

Consider the system of equations:
$$ x = x^2 + y^2 $$
$$ y = 3x^2y - y^3 $$
Find the number of distinct pairs $(x, y)$ of real numbers satisfying both equations.
**A.** 0  
**B.** 1  
**C.** 2  
**D.** 3  
**E.** 4

Determine the least positive integer that has exactly $1323$ distinct positive divisors and can be expressed in the form $m \cdot 30^k$. Here $m$ and $k$ are integers, and $30$ is not a divisor of $m$. Find the sum $m+k$.
A) 81
B) 82
C) 83
D) 84
E) 85

In a 3x3 grid where each row and column must contain one 'X', one 'Y', and one 'Z', if 'X' is placed at the center of the grid, and 'Y' must be placed in the top left corner, how many valid arrangements are possible?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 4\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 5$

Solve for the difference between the larger root and the smaller root of the quadratic equation $$(3 + 2\sqrt{2})x^2 + (5 + \sqrt{2})x - 4 = 0.$$
A) $\sqrt{177 - 122\sqrt{2}}$
B) $11 - 7\sqrt{2}$
C) $8\sqrt{2} - 12$
D) $11 - \sqrt{2}$

A square piece of paper has side length $2$ and vertices $A,B,C,$ and $D$ in that order. The paper is folded so that vertex $C$ meets edge $\overline{AD}$ at point $C'$, and edge $\overline{BC}$ intersects edge $\overline{AB}$ at point $E$. Suppose that $C'D = \frac{2}{3}$. What is the perimeter of triangle $\bigtriangleup AEC'$?
A) $\frac{17\sqrt{10} + 2\sqrt{37}}{12}$
B) $\frac{17\sqrt{10} + \sqrt{37}}{12}$
C) $\frac{20\sqrt{10} + \sqrt{35}}{12}$
D) $\frac{15\sqrt{10} + 3\sqrt{37}}{12}$

The graph below shows the total accumulated dollars (in millions) spent by the Surf City government during $1988$. For example, about $0.5$ million had been spent by the beginning of February and approximately $2$ million by the end of April. Approximately how many millions of dollars were spent during the first quarter months of January, February, and March?
$\text{(A)}\ 0.5 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 1.0 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 1.5 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 2.0 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 2.5$

Twelve chairs in a row are to be occupied by seven students and Professors Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta. These four professors arrive before the seven students and decide to choose their chairs so that each professor will be between two students and no two professors are adjacent to each other. In how many ways can Professors Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta choose their chairs?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 48 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 72 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 96 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 120 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 144$

The ratio of the areas of two concentric circles is $1:4$. If the radius of the smaller circle is $r$, then the difference between the radii of the two circles is best approximated by:
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0.5r \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ r \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 1.5r \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 2r \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 0.25r$

Line $m$ in the coordinate plane has equation $2x + 3y - 6 = 0$. This line is rotated $30^\circ$ clockwise about the point $(3, -2)$ to obtain line $n$. What is the $x$-coordinate of the $x$-intercept of line $n$?
A) $\frac{-2(3\sqrt{3} - 2)}{2\sqrt{3} + 3}$
B) $\frac{-2\sqrt{3} + 6}{2\sqrt{3} + 3}$
C) $\frac{2\sqrt{3} + 6}{3\sqrt{3} - 2}$
D) $\frac{(-2\sqrt{3} + 6)(3\sqrt{3} - 2)}{2\sqrt{3} + 3}$
E) $\frac{2(3\sqrt{3} - 2)}{2\sqrt{3} + 3}$

Determine the value(s) of $y$ for which the following pair of equations $x^2 + y^2 - 16 = 0$ and $x^2 - 3y - 12 = 0$ may have a common real solution.
$\textbf{(A) }-4\text{ only}\qquad \textbf{(B) }1\text{ only}\qquad \textbf{(C) }0, 4\qquad \textbf{(D) }-4, 1\qquad \textbf{(E) }\text{no } y$

For positive integers $n$, denote $D(n)$ as the number of pairs of different adjacent digits in the binary representation of $n$. For example, $D(5) = D(101_{2}) = 2$ and $D(44) = D(101100_{2}) = 3$. How many positive integers $n$ less than or equal to $55$ have $D(n) = 3$?
A) 8
B) 10
C) 11
D) 13
E) 15

A flat arrangement consisting of six squares labeled A, B, C, D, E, and F is folded along their edges to form a cube. Determine the label of the face opposite to the face labeled A, given that:
- Face B is adjacent to Face A.
- Face C and Face D are adjacent to each other and share a vertex with Face A.
- Face E and Face F share an edge with each other but not with Face A.
- A) B
- B) C
- C) D
- D) E
- E) F

In a physics test, $20\%$ of the students scored $60$ points, $40\%$ scored $75$ points, $25\%$ scored $85$ points, and the remaining students scored $95$ points. Calculate the difference between the mean and median score of the students' scores on this test.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 1.5\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 2.5\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 3.5$

Let $c$ be a constant. The simultaneous equations
$$
$$\begin{align*}
x-2y &= 5 \\
cx + 3y &= 2
\end{align*}$$
$$
have a solution $(x, y)$ in Quadrant I if and only if the constant $c$ satisfies which of the following conditions?
**A.** $c < \frac{2}{5}$ \\
**B.** $c > -\frac{3}{2}$ \\
**C.** $-\frac{3}{2} < c < \frac{2}{5}$ \\
**D.** $\frac{2}{5} < c < \frac{3}{2}$ \\
**E.** $c < \frac{3}{2}$

The first three terms of a geometric progression are $2^{1/4}, 2^{1/8}, 2^{1/16}$. Find the fourth term.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 2^{1/64} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 2^{1/32} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 2^{1/20} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 2^0 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 2^{1/24}$

The Riverdale Elementary School bookstore also sells pencils at a whole number of cents. Some eighth graders each bought a pencil, paying a total of $2.34 dollars. Some of the $50$ fifth graders each bought a pencil, and they paid a total of $3.25 dollars. How many more fifth graders than eighth graders bought a pencil?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 5 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 6 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 7 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 8 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 9$

Mandy is choosing between buying a laptop from two different stores. Store X offers a $20\%$ discount on the sticker price and then a $120$ rebate, while store Y gives a flat $35\%$ discount with no rebate. Mandy finds that she would save $10 by purchasing the laptop from store X rather than store Y. What is the original sticker price of the laptop?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 700\qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ 733\qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ 750\qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ 800$

Find the smallest whole number that is larger than the sum
$$3\dfrac{1}{3} + 4\dfrac{1}{4} + 6\dfrac{1}{6} + 7\dfrac{1}{7}.$$
A) 19
B) 20
C) 21
D) 22
E) 23

Distinct points $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ lie on the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=16$ and have integer coordinates. The distances $AB$ and $CD$ are irrational numbers. What is the greatest possible value of the ratio $\frac{AB}{CD}$?
A) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
B) $1$
C) $\sqrt{2}$
D) $2$
E) $\sqrt{3}$

In John's first $6$ basketball games, he scored $10, 5, 8, 6, 11,$ and $4$ points. In his seventh game, he scored fewer than $15$ points and his points-per-game average for the seven games was an integer. For the eighth game, he also scored fewer than $15$ points, and the average for the $8$ games became an integer again. What is the product of the number of points he scored in the seventh and eighth games?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 24\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 30\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 35\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 42\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 50$

An equilateral triangle with a side length of 12 is partitioned into 9 congruent triangles, 6 of which are shaded. Calculate the shaded area.
A) $12\sqrt{3}$
B) $18\sqrt{3}$
C) $24\sqrt{3}$
D) $30\sqrt{3}$
E) $36\sqrt{3}$

Let $n$ be the product of three consecutive integers, and it is known that $n$ is divisible by $5$. Which of the following is not necessarily a divisor of $n$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 10 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 15 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 20 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 30 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 60$
$\textbf{(A)}\ 10 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 15 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 20 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 30 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 60$

A circle's radius is increased by 50%, and its circumference is also increased by 50%. By what percentage is the area of the circle increased?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 75\%\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 100\%\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 125\%\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 150\%\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 175\%$

Given that all the roots of the polynomial $z^3 + pz^2 + qz + r = 0$ are complex numbers lying on a circle in the complex plane centered at $0+0i$ with radius $\frac{1}{2}$, find the sum of the reciprocals of the roots. Which of the following must this sum necessarily be?
- No choices provided yet.
**A)** $-2p$  
**B)** $p$  
**C)** $-q$  
**D)** $-2q$

In Paul's first $6$ soccer matches, he scored $2, 5, 4, 3, 6,$ and $2$ goals respectively. In his seventh match, he scored fewer than $10$ goals, and the goals-per-game average for the seven matches was an integer. Similarly, in his eighth match, he scored fewer than $10$ goals, and the goals-per-game average for the $8$ matches was also an integer. What is the product of the number of goals he scored in the seventh and eighth matches?
A) 20
B) 24
C) 30
D) 36
E) 42

A $9 \times 9 \times 9$ cube is divided into twenty-seven $3 \times 3 \times 3$ smaller cubes. From each $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cube, the eight corner unit cubes are removed. Calculate the surface area of the resulting structure.
A) 960
B) 1024
C) 1056
D) 1152
E) 1248

Tetrahedron $PQRS$ has $PQ = 6$, $PR = 4$, $PS = 5$, $QR = 5$, $QS = 4$, and $RS = \frac{15}{4}\sqrt{2}$. Calculate the volume of tetrahedron $PQRS$.
A) $3\sqrt{2}$
B) $2\sqrt{5}$
C) $ \frac{24}{5} $
D) $3\sqrt{3}$
E) $ \frac{24}{5}\sqrt{2} $

Using only pennies, nickels, quarters, and half-dollars, what is the smallest number of coins Freddie would need so he could pay any amount of money less than a dollar?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 7\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 8\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 10\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 11$

Let $f(z) = 3i\overline{z}$. The polynomial $$P(z) = z^4 - 2z^3 + 6z^2 - 3z + 2$$ has four complex roots: $z_1$, $z_2$, $z_3$, and $z_4$. Define another polynomial $$Q(z) = z^4 + Az^3 + Bz^2 + Cz + D$$, where the roots are $f(z_1)$, $f(z_2)$, $f(z_3)$, and $f(z_4)$. Determine $B + D$.
A) 54
B) 108
C) 162
D) 216

Let circle $O$ have a radius $r$. Two semicircles, $POQ$ and $ROS$, are positioned such that their straight diameters lie along chords of circle $O$. Semicircle $POQ$ has a radius $r_1 = r/\sqrt{2}$, and semicircle $ROS$ has a radius $r_2 = r/2$. What is the ratio of the combined areas of semicircles $POQ$ and $ROS$ to the area of circle $O$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{4}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{3}{8}\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{2}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{3}\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{1}{8}$

Let $f(x) = x^2 + 5x + 6$. Determine the number of integers $s$ in the set $S = \{5, 6, 7, \ldots, 30\}$ such that $f(s)$ has remainder zero when divided by $8$.
A) 6
B) 7
C) 8
D) 9
E) 10

Thirty percent less than 80 is one-fourth more than what number?
A) 42
B) 44
C) 45
D) 48

At Pythagoras High School, $256$ students participated in a referendum about three issues. The results were as follows: $185$ students supported the first issue, $142$ students supported the second issue, and there were $37$ students who opposed all three issues. If $105$ students supported both the first and the second issues, how many students supported only one of the two issues?
$\textbf{(A) }110\qquad\textbf{(B) }117\qquad\textbf{(C) }122\qquad\textbf{(D) }130\qquad\textbf{(E) }135$

Determine the number of distinct points in the $xy$-plane where the graphs of $(x + 2y - 10)(x - 4y + 8) = 0$ and $(2x - y - 1)(5x + 3y - 15) = 0$ intersect.
A) 1
B) 2
C) 3
D) 4

Jessica's empty swimming pool holds $36,000$ gallons of water when full. She uses $6$ hoses to fill it, each supplying $3$ gallons of water per minute. How many hours will it take to fill Jessica's pool?
$\textbf{(A)}\  32  \qquad \textbf{(B)}\   33  \qquad \textbf{(C)}\  33 \frac{1}{3}   \qquad \textbf{(D)}\  34   \qquad \textbf{(E)}\   35$

A biased coin with a probability $\frac{4}{5}$ of landing heads and a second fair coin are tossed alternatingly, starting with the biased coin, for a total of 40 tosses. What is the probability that the total number of heads is even?
A) $\frac{1}{2}$
B) $\frac{1}{2}(1 - (\frac{2}{5})^{40})$
C) $\frac{1}{2}(1 + (\frac{2}{5})^{40})$
D) $(\frac{2}{5})^{40}$

Determine the formula expressing the relationship between $x$ and $y$ in the table:
$$$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline x & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7\\ \hline y & 12 & 22 & 36 & 54 & 76\\ \hline\end{array}$$$$
$$
\textbf{(A)}\ y = x^3 + x^2 + 5x + 9\qquad
\textbf{(B)}\ y = x^3 - 3x^2 + 5x + 9\qquad
\textbf{(C)}\ y = x^3 - x^2 + x + 1\qquad
\textbf{(D)}\ y = x^3 - 2x^2 + 4x + 7\qquad

$$

A worker's salary is reduced by 30%. What percentage increase is needed on the new salary to restore it to the original salary?

Define the sequence $\{b_n\}$ by

$b_{n+1} = b_n + 3n$
for $n \geq 1$.
Determine $b_{100}$.
A) 14850

Three balls are independently tossed into bins numbered with positive integers. For each ball, the probability it lands in bin $i$ is $3^{-i}$, where $i=1,2,3,...$. More than one ball can be placed in each bin. Calculate the probability that the balls are placed into bins such that their positions form an arithmetic progression with a difference of two bins between each consecutive ball. The probability should be expressed in simplest form as $\frac{p}{q}$, where $p$ and $q$ are coprime integers. What is $p+q?$
A. 9457
B. 9467
C. 9477
D. 9487

Given the progression $8^{\frac{2}{11}}, 8^{\frac{3}{11}}, 8^{\frac{4}{11}}, \dots, 8^{\frac{(n+1)}{11}}$, the least positive integer $n$ such that the product of the first $n$ terms of the progression exceeds 1,000,000.
- **(A)** 9
- **(B)** 10
- **(C)** 11
- **(D)** 12
- **(E)** 13

There is also face D connected to face B at one edge, face E connected to face C at another edge, and a face X connected to face A. Which of the lettered faces is opposite the face marked X when the net is folded into a cube?
$\mathrm{(A) \ A } \qquad \mathrm{(B) \ B } \qquad \mathrm{(C) \ C } \qquad \mathrm{(D) \ D } \qquad \mathrm{(E) \ E }$

Which of these operations results in the largest value?

The number $15!$ has a certain number of positive integer divisors. One of them is chosen at random. What is the probability that it is odd?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{12} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ \frac{1}{15} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{10} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{6} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \frac{1}{20}$

The line $10x + 4y = 40$ forms a triangle with the coordinate axes. What is the sum of the lengths of the altitudes of this triangle?

Thirteen chairs in a row are to be occupied by eight students and Professors Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta. These four professors arrive before the eight students and decide to choose their chairs so that each professor will be between two students. In how many ways can Professors Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta choose their chairs?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 1260$  
$\textbf{(B)}\ 1680$  
$\textbf{(C)}\ 2520$  
$\textbf{(D)}\ 3360$  
$\textbf{(E)}\ 5040$

An equilateral triangle $ABC$ has an area of 27. The rays trisecting $\angle BAC$ intersect $BC$ at $D$ and $E$. What is the area of $\triangle ADE$?
A. $3\sqrt{3}$
B. $4\sqrt{3}$
C. $5\sqrt{3}$
D. $6\sqrt{3}$
E. $7\sqrt{3}$

Tom now has a collection of 15 snakes, 5 of which are green and 6 of which are cheerful. He observes that:
- all of his cheerful snakes can multiply,
- none of his green snakes can divide, and
- all of his snakes that can't divide also can't multiply.

Which conclusion can be correctly drawn about Tom's snakes?
(Solution to follow)
$\textbf{(A) }$ Green snakes can multiply.
$\textbf{(B) }$ Green snakes are cheerful.
$\textbf{(C) }$ Snakes that can multiply are green.
$\textbf{(D) }$ Cheerful snakes are not green.
$\textbf{(E) }$ Cheerful snakes can't divide.
ch: $ Snakes that can multiply are green.

Alvin, Betty, and Carl were discussing their proximity to the nearest lake during a picnic. Alvin claimed, "The lake is at least 10 miles away," Betty asserted, "The lake is at most 9 miles away," and Carl declared, "The lake is exactly at 7 miles distance." Later, it was determined that none of these statements were correct. Let $ d $ be the distance in miles to the nearest lake. Determine the set of all possible values of $ d $.
- **(A)** $ (7, 9) $
- **(B)** $ (9, \infty) $
- **(C)** $ (9, 10) $
- **(D)** $ [0, 7) \cup (7, 9] $
- **(E)** $ (10, \infty) $

Consider a pyramid made of cubical blocks arranged in layers. The first layer (bottom) contains 15 blocks arranged in a specific pattern. On top of this, a second layer of 10 blocks is arranged, each resting on three blocks from the first layer. The third layer contains 6 blocks, again each resting on three from the second layer. The fourth layer has 3 blocks, and finally, the topmost fifth layer has a single block. 
Each block in layers 2 to 5 is assigned a number which is the sum of the numbers assigned to the three blocks directly below it. The blocks in the bottom layer are numbered 1 through 15 in some order. Determine the smallest possible number which could be assigned to the top block.
A) 120
B) 135
C) 150
D) 165

How many three-digit numbers are divisible by 17?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 50\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 52\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 53\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 55\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 60$

Simplify $\left(\sqrt[3]{32} - \sqrt{4 \frac{1}{4}}\right)^2$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{(8\sqrt[3]{2} - \sqrt{17})^2}{4} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 16\sqrt[3]{2} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \frac{(8\sqrt[3]{2} + \sqrt{17})^2}{4}\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 8\sqrt[3]{2}\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \sqrt{17}$

Circle $O$ has diameters $AB$ and $CD$ perpendicular to each other. $AN$ is any chord intersecting $CD$ at $Q$ and $AB$ at $N$. If $N$ is the midpoint of $AB$, find the product $AQ \cdot AN$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ r^2 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 2r^2\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 4r^2 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 3r^2\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \frac{r^2}{2}$

Determine the units digit of the decimal expansion of $$\left(17 + \sqrt{224}\right)^{21} + \left(17 + \sqrt{224}\right)^{85}.$$
- **(A)** 0
- **(B)** 2
- **(C)** 5
- **(D)** 8
- **(E)** 9

Values for $A,B,C,$ and $D$ are to be selected from $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$ without replacement (i.e., no two letters can have the same value). How many ways can these values be assigned such that the curves $y=Ax^2+B$ and $y=Cx^2+D$ intersect at some $x$ where $x \geq 0$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 105 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 210 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 315 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 420 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 525$

For each positive integer $n > 1$, let $Q(n)$ denote the greatest prime factor of $n$. For how many positive integers $n$ is it true that both $Q(n) = \sqrt{n}$ and $Q(3n + 16) = \sqrt{3n + 16}$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 4$

For integers $ l $ and $ m $ such that $ 1 \le l < m $, let $ C^m_l = \frac{m!}{l!(m-l)!} $. Evaluate if the expression $ \left(\frac{m - 3l + 2}{l+2}\right)C^m_l $ is an integer:
- **(A)** for all $ l $ and $ m $
- **(B)** if $ m $ is divisible by $ l+2 $
- **(C)** for all odd values of $ l $ and $ m $
- **(D)** for all even values of $ l $ and $ m $
- **(E)** if $ m $ is divisible by 3
- **(A)** for all $ l $ and $ m $
- **(B)** if $ m $ is divisible by $ l+2 $
- **(C)** for all odd values of $ l $ and $ m $
- **(D)** for all even values of $ l $ and $ m $
- **(E)** if $ m $ is divisible by 3

In a certain population, the ratio of the number of men to the number of women is $7$ to $8$. If the average (arithmetic mean) age of the men is $36$ and the average age of the women is $30$, then the average age of the population is:
$\textbf{(A)}\ 32\frac{4}{5}\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 33\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 32\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 34\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 31\frac{4}{5}$

A courier travels for 3 hours at 65 miles per hour. The car uses 28 miles per gallon of gasoline. She receives $0.55 per mile as her payment, and her gasoline costs $2.50 per gallon. Calculate her net rate of pay per hour after deducting her gasoline expenses.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 20\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 25\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 28\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 30\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 32$

If the distance light travels in one year is approximately $9,460,800,000,000$ kilometers, calculate the distance light travels in $70$ years.
**A.** $6.62256 \times 10^{14}$ kilometers 
**B.** $9.4608 \times 10^{12}$ kilometers 
**C.** $6.62256 \times 10^{15}$ kilometers 
**D.** $9.4608 \times 10^{14}$ kilometers

Find the slope of the line $\frac{x}{4} + \frac{y}{3} = 1$.
A) $-\frac{3}{4}$  
B) $-\frac{4}{3}$  
C) $\frac{3}{4}$  
D) $\frac{4}{3}$

Suppose $9^x - 9^{x-1} = 72$, find $(3x)^x$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 18 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 24 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 32 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 36 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 48$

Heather is comparing the price of a new laptop at two different shops. Shop A offers a $ 20\% $ discount on the sticker price followed by a $ \$120 $ rebate, while Shop B offers $ 30\% $ off the same sticker price with no rebate. Heather finds that she saves $ \$25 $ by purchasing from Shop A. What is the sticker price of the laptop, in dollars?
A. $ \$800 $
B. $ \$900 $
C. $ \$950 $
D. $ \$1000 $
E. $ \$1050 $

Circles $A, B, C,$ and $D$ each have radius 2, except $D$ which has a radius of 1. Circles $A$ and $B$ share one point of tangency. Circle $C$ has a point of tangency with the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$. Circle $D$ is externally tangent to Circle $C$ and internally tangent to both Circles $A$ and $B$ simultaneously. Determine the area inside circle $C$ but outside circles $A$, $B$, and $D$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 4 - \pi \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 4 - 0.5 \pi \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 5 - \pi \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 3.5$

Every fourth-grade classroom at Pine Hill Elementary has $20$ students, $2$ pet rabbits, and $1$ pet hamster. How many more students than pets are there in all $5$ of the fourth-grade classrooms?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 75\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 80\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 85\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 90\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 95$

A cylinder with a radius of 8 cm and a height of 12 cm is divided into three equal wedges along its height by slicing through the cylinder. What is the volume of one of these wedges?
$\textbf{(A)} \ 300 \qquad \textbf{(B)} \ 500 \qquad \textbf{(C)} \ 800 \qquad \textbf{(D)} \ 804 \qquad \textbf{(E)} \ 1000$

In the adjoining figure $ABCD$ is a square and $CMN$ is a right triangle with $MN$ and $CN$ as the legs and $CM$ as the hypotenuse. If the area of $ABCD$ is one square inch, then the area of $CMN$ in square inches is:
A) $\frac{1}{4}$
B) $\frac{1}{8}$
C) $\frac{1}{16}$
D) $\frac{1}{2}$

What is the value of $\dfrac{\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{5}}{\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}}$?
$\text{(A) } \frac 14 \qquad \text{(B) } \frac 23 \qquad \text{(C) } \frac 12 \qquad \text{(D) } \frac 34 \qquad \text{(E) } \frac 35$

A small hotel has 4 rooms, and 6 friends arrive to spend the night. The friends can room in any combination they wish, but with no more than 3 friends per room. In how many ways can the hotel manager assign the guests to the rooms?
A) 1480
B) 1560
C) 1620
D) 1740

Using only nickels, dimes, and quarters, what is the smallest number of coins Sarah would need to be able to pay any amount of money less than a dollar?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 8 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 9 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 10 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 12 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 15$

A large wooden cube, with each side of length $n$ units, is completely painted blue and then cut into $n^3$ smaller unit cubes. We need to figure out $n$ such that exactly one-third of the faces of all the unit cubes combined are blue. What is $n$?
A) 2
B) 3
C) 4
D) 5
E) 6

In a rectangular parallelepiped, let $AB = 4$, $BC = 2$, and $CG = 3$. Point $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{EF}$. Determine the volume of the pyramid with base $BCHE$ and apex $M$.
$\textbf{(A) } 4 \qquad \textbf{(B) } \sqrt{20} \qquad \textbf{(C) } 5 \qquad \textbf{(D) } 3 \qquad \textbf{(E)} \frac{3}{2}$

Consider the set $\{2, 5, 8, 11, 14, 17, 20\}$. How many different integers can be expressed as the sum of two distinct members from this set plus 6, where the resulting sum must also be a multiple of 4?
$\text{(A)}\ 3 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 7 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 12 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 15 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 18$

Kevin was born on Leap Day, February 29, 2000, which occurred on a Tuesday. On what day of the week will Kevin celebrate his Leap Day birthday on February 29, 2024?
$\textbf{(A)}$ Saturday
$\textbf{(B)}$ Sunday
$\textbf{(C)}$ Monday
$\textbf{(D)}$ Tuesday
$\textbf{(E)}$ Wednesday

Convex quadrilateral $ABCD$ has $AB = 8$ and $CD = 18$. Diagonals $AC$ and $BD$ intersect at $E$. If $AC = 20$ and $\triangle AED$ and $\triangle BEC$ have equal areas, what is the length of $AE$?
A) $\frac{60}{13}$
B) $\frac{72}{13}$
C) $\frac{80}{13}$
D) $\frac{90}{13}$
E) $\frac{100}{13}$

Assume Al, Bob, and Cody are randomly assigned distinct whole numbers from 1 to 15 inclusive. What is the probability that Al's number is a perfect square multiple of Bob's number, and Bob's number is a divisor of Cody's number?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \dfrac{1}{70} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ \dfrac{1}{80} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \dfrac{1}{61} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \dfrac{1}{100} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \dfrac{1}{40}$

A rectangular floor measures $a$ by $b$ feet, where $a$ and $b$ are positive integers. An artist paints a rectangle on the floor, parallel to the sides of the floor, such that a uniform unpainted border remains around the painted rectangle. The width of the border is $2$ feet and the unpainted area occupies $\frac{2}{3}$ of the area of the entire floor. How many possibilities are there for the ordered pair $(a,b)$ given $a\geq5$ and $b > a$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 4\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 6$

How many 9-digit palindromes can be formed using the digits 1, 1, 3, 3, 5, 5, 7, 7, 7?
$\text{(A)}\ 6$
$\text{(B)}\ 12$
$\text{(C)}\ 24$
$\text{(D)}\ 36$
$\text{(E)}\ 48$

The number of roots satisfying the equation $\sqrt{9 - x} = x\sqrt{9 - x}$, where $x \in \mathbb{R}$ or $x \in \mathbb{C}$, is:
A. 0   
B. 1   
C. 2   
D. 3   
E. 4

A train travels the first part of its journey for $x$ miles at 50 mph and the second part for $3x$ miles at 80 mph. Calculate the total time taken for the entire journey.
A. $\frac{11x}{200}$ hours

$\mathrm{(A)}\ \text{the empty set}\qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \text{one line}\qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \text{two lines}\qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \text{a circle}\qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ \text{the entire plane}$
$\mathrm{(A)}\ \text{the empty set}\qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \text{one line}\qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \text{two lines}\qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \text{a circle}\qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ \text{the entire plane}$

\text{the entire plane}$

What is the value of $3 - (-3)^{-3} - 1$?
$\textbf{(A) } \frac{53}{27}\qquad\textbf{(B) } \frac{54}{27}\qquad\textbf{(C) } \frac{55}{27}\qquad\textbf{(D) } \frac{56}{27}$

John divides a circle into 16 sectors where the central angles of these sectors are integers and form an arithmetic sequence. What is the degree measure of the smallest possible sector angle?
A) 10 degrees
B) 12 degrees
C) 15 degrees
D) 18 degrees
E) 20 degrees

A square lawn is partitioned into different garden plots - A, B, C, D, and E. Each unit of the square grid is 1 square meter, and some garden plots have triangular extensions or cuts. Find the garden plot with the largest area.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 10\ \text{square meters}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 11\ \text{square meters}\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 10.5\ \text{square meters}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 12\ \text{square meters}\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 12\ \text{square meters}$

In triangle $ABC$, $AC=2 \cdot AB$. Points $D$ and $E$ lie on $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$, respectively, such that $\angle BAE = \angle ACD$. Let $F$ be the intersection of segments $AE$ and $CD$, and suppose $\triangle AFE$ is isosceles with $AF=FE$, and that $\triangle CFE$ is equilateral. Find the measure of $\angle ACB$.
A) $30^\circ$
B) $45^\circ$
C) $60^\circ$
D) $75^\circ$
E) $90^\circ$

The diagonal of square $A$ is $2c+d$. Find the area of square $B$, which has three times the area of square $A$.
A) $\frac{3(2c+d)^2}{4}$  
B) $(2c+d)^2$  
C) $3(2c+d)^2$  
D) $\frac{3(2c+d)^2}{2}$  
E) $2(2c+d)^2$

What is the value of
$$\left(\left((3+1)^{-1} \cdot 2\right)^{-1} \cdot 2\right)^{-1} \cdot 2?$$
$\textbf{(A) } \frac23 \qquad \textbf{(B) } \frac12 \qquad \textbf{(C) } \frac34 \qquad \textbf{(D) } \frac45 \qquad \textbf{(E) } \frac56$

Liam has two identical 12-ounce cups. Initially, he places 8 ounces of tea into the first cup and in the second cup, he places 8 ounces of milk. He transfers 1/4th of the tea from the first cup into the second cup and then stirs the mixture in the second cup thoroughly. After stirring, Liam transfers 1/3rd of the mixture from the second cup back to the first cup. What fraction of the liquid in the first cup is now milk?
A) $\frac{1}{4}$
B) $\frac{1}{3}$
C) $\frac{1}{2}$
D) $\frac{2}{5}$
E) $\frac{3}{4}$

A calculator has only two keys [+1] and [x3]. Starting with "1" on the display, what is the fewest number of keystrokes needed to reach "243"?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 4\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 6\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 7\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 8$

Jack and Jill plan to visit a park that is 2 miles away from their house. Jill cycles to the park at a constant speed of 12 miles per hour, while Jack jogs there at a constant speed of 5 miles per hour. How many minutes before Jack does Jill arrive at the park?
$\textbf{(A) }10\qquad\textbf{(B) }12\qquad\textbf{(C) }14\qquad\textbf{(D) }16\qquad \textbf{(E) }18$

At Cityview Elementary, the school store sells erasers at a consistent whole number price. Each fifth grader bought an eraser, collectively spending $3.25. $\textbf{(A)}\  25  \qquad \textbf{(B)}\  26 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\  27  \qquad \textbf{(D)}\   28  \qquad \textbf{(E)}\   29$

What is the value of $$\frac{3^{2020} - 3^{2018}}{3^{2020} + 3^{2018}}?$$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{4}{5}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{3}{2}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 2021\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 3^{4036}$

Determine how many ordered pairs of integers $(x, y)$ satisfy the equation $$x^{4} + y^2 = 3y + 3.$$
$\textbf{(A) } 0 \qquad\textbf{(B) } 1 \qquad\textbf{(C) } 2 \qquad\textbf{(D) } 4 \qquad\textbf{(E) } \text{infinitely many}$

In a right trapezoid, one of the non-rectangular vertex angles is divided into angles $x^\circ$ and $y^\circ$, where both angles are prime numbers. If the angle at this vertex totals $90^\circ$, find the least possible value of $y^\circ$.
$\textbf{(A)}\  2 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 3 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\  5 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 7 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 11$

A $10$ by $4$ rectangle shares the same center as a circle with a radius of $3$. There is also an equilateral triangle with a side length of $6$, one vertex of which is at the center of the circle and rectangle. Calculate the area of the region common to the rectangle and the circle.
A) $3\pi$
B) $6\pi$
C) $9\pi$
D) $12\pi$
E) $15\pi$

Distinct points $P$, $Q$, $R$, and $S$ lie on a line, with $PQ = 1$, $QR = 2$, and $RS = 3$. Points $T$ and $U$ are on another line parallel to the first, with $TU = 2$. A triangle with positive area has three of the six points as its vertices. What are the possible values for the area of such a triangle?
$\text{(A) } 3 \qquad \text{(B) } 4 \qquad \text{(C) } 5 \qquad \text{(D) } 6 \qquad \text{(E) } 7$

Let $a, a', b, b',$ and $c$ be real numbers with $a$ and $a'$ nonzero. The solution to $ax + b = c$ is greater than the solution to $a'x + b' = c$ if and only if:
$\text{(A) } a'c > ac' \quad \text{(B) } ac' < a'c \quad \text{(C) } ac > a'c' \quad \text{(D) } \frac{c-b}{a} < \frac{c-b'}{a'} \quad \text{(E) } \frac{c-b'}{a'} < \frac{c-b}{a}$
- (A) $a'c > ac'$
- (B) $ac' < a'c$
- (C) $ac > a'c'$
- (D) $\frac{c-b}{a} < \frac{c-b'}{a'}$
- (E) $\frac{c-b'}{a'} < \frac{c-b}{a}$

In triangle $ \triangle ABC $, $ \angle ABC $ is a right angle. The sides $ AB $, $ AC $, and $ BC $ serve as the diameters of three respective semicircles. A) $ \frac{\sqrt{500}}{2} $

In Ms. Gregory's class, $\frac{3}{4}$ of the boys and $\frac{2}{3}$ of the girls passed a science quiz, with an equal number of boys and girls passing the quiz. Each student in the class also participates in one of two clubs: Science or Math. The number of students participating in the Math club is twice the number of those in the Science club. $\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{16} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ -\frac{1}{64} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{64} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{32} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ -\frac{1}{16}$

On Monday, Lila puts three quarts of seeds, $30\%$ of which are sunflower seeds, into a garden feeder. On each successive day, she adds another three quarts of the same mix of seeds without removing any remaining seeds. Each day, the wildlife consumes only $20\%$ of the sunflower seeds and all of the other seeds. Additionally, $10\%$ of the unconsumed sunflower seeds naturally decay by the next morning. On which day, just after Lila has added the seeds, will wildlife find that more than half the seeds in the feeder are sunflower seeds?
A) Tuesday
B) Wednesday
C) Thursday
D) Friday
E) Saturday

In Mr. Arith's class, there are 40 students, and more than half bought markers at the school store. Every student who bought markers purchased the same number of markers, which was an even number greater than 2. Each marker's cost in cents was higher than the number of markers each student purchased, and the total expenditure for all markers was $3185$ cents. What was the cost of each marker in cents?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 10 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 13 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 17 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 23 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 27$

The altitude drawn to the base of an isosceles triangle is $10$, and the perimeter is $40$. Find the area of the triangle.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 70\qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ 75\qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ 80\qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ 85\qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ 90$

Let $f(n) = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n}$, where $n$ is a positive integer and $x_k = (-1)^{k+1}$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. Determine the set of possible values of $f(n)$.
$\text{(A) } \{0\}$
$\text{(B) } \{\frac{1}{n}\}$
$\text{(C) } \{0, \frac{1}{n}\}$
$\text{(D) } \{\frac{1}{n}, -\frac{1}{n}\}$
$\text{(E) } \{0, -\frac{1}{n}\}$

Each of the $6$ sides and the $9$ diagonals of a regular hexagon are randomly and independently colored red or blue with equal probability. What is the probability that there exists at least one triangle whose vertices are among the vertices of the hexagon such that all of its sides are the same color?
- A) $\frac{991}{1000}$
- B) $\frac{253}{256}$
- C) $\frac{995}{1000}$

$\textbf{(A) } 2 \text{ mph} \qquad \textbf{(B) } 4 \text{ mph} \qquad \textbf{(C) } 6 \text{ mph} \qquad \textbf{(D) } 8 \text{ mph} \qquad \textbf{(E) } 10 \text{ mph}$

Two jars each contain the same number of marbles, and every marble is either blue or green. In Jar $1$, the ratio of blue to green marbles is $7:3$, and in Jar $2$, the ratio of blue to green marbles is $8:1$. There are $110$ green marbles in all. How many more blue marbles are in Jar $1$ than in Jar $2$?
$\textbf{(A) } 20\qquad\textbf{(B) } 35 \qquad\textbf{(C) } 45 \qquad\textbf{(D) } 51 \qquad \textbf{(E) } 60$

The reciprocal of $\left( \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}\right)$ is
(A) $\frac{5}{12}$  
(B) $\frac{12}{6}$  
(C) $\frac{10}{12}$  
(D) $\frac{12}{5}$  
(E) $\frac{24}{5}$

In Ms. Spark's class, half of the boys and two-thirds of the girls passed a science test. Additionally, the number of boys who failed the test is twice the number of girls who failed. What is the minimum possible number of students in the class?
A) 5
B) 6
C) 7
D) 9
E) 12

Determine the equation that represents the relationship between $x$ and $y$ as shown in the accompanying table:
$$$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline x&0&1&2&3&4\\\hline y&0&-15&-40&-75&-120\\\hline\end{array}$$$$
A. $y = -5x^2 - 10x$  
B. $y = -5x^2 - 15x$  
C. $y = -4x^2 - 10x$  
D. $y = -5x^2 - 5x$  
E. None of these

An acronym XYZ is drawn within a 2x4 rectangular grid with grid lines spaced 1 unit apart. The letter X is formed by two diagonals crossing in a $1 \times 1$ square. Y consists of a vertical line segment and two slanted segments each forming 45° with the vertical line, making up a symmetric letter. Z is formed by a horizontal segment at the top and bottom of a $1 \times 2$ rectangle, with a diagonal connecting these segments. In units, what is the total length of the line segments forming the acronym XYZ?
A) $5 + 4\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{5}$
B) $5 + 2\sqrt{2} + 3\sqrt{5}$
C) $6 + 3\sqrt{2} + 2\sqrt{5}$
D) $7 + 4\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{3}$
E) $4 + 5\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{5}$

In a collection containing only hexagonal and square tiles, there are a total of $30$ tiles and $128$ edges. How many square tiles are in the collection?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 20 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 22 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 24 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 26 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 28$

In the figure below, $N$ congruent semicircles rest on the diameter of a large semicircle. Their diameters fully cover the diameter of the large semicircle without any gaps. Let $A$ represent the total area of all the small semicircles, and $B$ represent the area of the region within the large semicircle but outside the small semicircles. If the ratio $A:B$ is $1:12$, determine the value of $N$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 11 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 12 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 13 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 14 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 15$

Three cubes of the same size are to be painted independently on each face. Each face of each cube can be randomly painted one of three colors: red, blue, or green. What is the probability that after they are painted, all three cubes can be rotated to look exactly the same?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{123}{81,900,081} \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{211}{129,140,163} \qquad\textbf{(C)}\  \frac{502}{345,600,234} \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{312}{204,857,609} \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{789}{524,288,000} $

In a modified version of the AMC 8 contest, Jennifer answers 15 questions correctly, answers 10 questions incorrectly, and doesn't answer the remaining 5 questions out of a total of 30 questions. In this version of the contest, each correct answer awards 2 points, each incorrect answer deducts 1 point, and unanswered questions do not affect the score. What is Jennifer's score?
A) 5
B) 15
C) 20
D) 25
E) 30

Consider a polynomial $Q(x) = x^3 + dx^2 + ex + f$ whose roots are $\cos \frac{\pi}{9}, \cos \frac{2\pi}{9},$ and $\cos \frac{4\pi}{9}$. Find the product $def$.
A) $-\frac{1}{16}$
B) $0$
C) $\frac{1}{32}$
D) $-\frac{1}{32}$

The sale ad now reads: "Buy three tires at the regular price and get the fourth tire for 25% off." Sam paid $270 for a set of four tires at the sale. What was the regular price of one tire?
$\text{(A)}\ 70\text{ dollars} \qquad \text{(B)}\ 72\text{ dollars} \qquad \text{(C)}\ 75\text{ dollars} \qquad \text{(D)}\ 80\text{ dollars} \qquad \text{(E)}\ 85\text{ dollars}$

For any positive integer $m$, define $\boxed{m}$ to be the sum of the positive factors of $m$. 
Given this, compute $\boxed{\boxed{17}}$.
A) 36
B) 37
C) 38
D) 39
E) 40

A printing press starts operating at $\text{9:00}\ {\small\text{AM}}$ and by $\text{12:00}\ {\small\text{PM}}$, it has printed half of the day's newspaper order. At what time will the printing press finish the entire order?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ \text{2:00}\ {\small\text{PM}}\qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \text{3:00}\ {\small\text{PM}}\qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \text{4:00}\ {\small\text{PM}}\qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \text{5:00}\ {\small\text{PM}}$

Given points $P(-2,-3)$ and $Q(5,3)$ in the $xy$-plane; point $R(2,m)$ is taken so that $PR+RQ$ is a minimum. What is the value of $m$?
A) $\frac{3}{7}$
B) $\frac{2}{7}$
C) $\frac{1}{7}$
D) $-\frac{3}{7}$

Determine the number of positive integers $n$ such that $n^3 - 7n^2 + 16n - 12$ is a prime number.
A. zero
B. one
C. two
D. three
E. more than three

Mark has seven green cards numbered 1 through 7 and five red cards numbered 2 through 6. He arranges the cards so that colors alternate, and the sum of the numbers on each pair of neighboring cards (one red, one green) forms a prime number. What is the sum of the numbers on the last three cards in his stack?
A) 14
B) 15
C) 16
D) 17
E) 18

Let $T_1$ be a triangle with side lengths $2002, 2004,$ and $2006$. For $n \ge 1$, if $T_n = \triangle ABC$ and $D, E,$ and $F$ are the points of tangency of the incircle of $\triangle ABC$ to the sides $AB, BC$, and $AC,$ respectively, then $T_{n+1}$ is a triangle with side lengths $AD, BE,$ and $CF,$ if it exists. Find the perimeter of the last triangle in the sequence $( T_n )$.
A) $\frac{1506}{64}$
B) $\frac{1506}{128}$
C) $\frac{1506}{256}$
D) $\frac{1506}{512}$
E) $\frac{1506}{1024}$

Determine which of these five numbers $\sqrt{9},\,\sqrt[3]{0.512},\,\sqrt[4]{0.0625},\,\sqrt[3]{-8}\cdot \sqrt{(0.25)^{-1}}$, is (are) rational:
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{none}\qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ \text{all}\qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ \text{the first, second, and fourth}\qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ \text{only the first and fourth}\qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ \text{only the first, second, and third}$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{none}\qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ \text{all}\qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ \text{the first, second, and fourth}\qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ \text{only the first and fourth}\qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ \text{only the first, second, and third}$
ch:
\text{only the first, second, and third}$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{none}\qquad  \textbf{(B)}\ \text{all}\qquad  \textbf{(C)}\ \text{the first, second, and fourth}\qquad  \textbf{(D)}\ \text{only the first and fourth}\qquad  \textbf{(E)}\ \text{only the first, second, and third}

p:
A merchant bought some goods at a discount of 30% off the list price. He wishes to mark them at a price such that after offering a discount of 10% on the marked price, he still makes a profit of 25% on the selling price. What percent of the list price should the marked price be?
A) 95%
B) 100%
C) 103.7%
D) 110%
E) 120%

A square piece of paper with side length 2 has vertices $A, B, C,$ and $D$ in that order. The paper is folded such that vertex $A$ meets edge $\overline{BC}$ at point $A'$. If $A'C = \frac{1}{2}$, determine the perimeter of triangle $\triangle A'BD$.
$\textbf{(A)} \frac{3+\sqrt{17}}{2} + 1\sqrt{2}$
$\textbf{(B)} \frac{3+\sqrt{17}}{2} + 2\sqrt{2}$
$\textbf{(C)} \frac{3+\sqrt{17}}{2} + 3\sqrt{2}$
$\textbf{(D)} \frac{2}{\sqrt{17}} + 2\sqrt{2}$
$\textbf{(E)} 5+ \sqrt{2}$

Evaluate $(x^x)^{(x^x)}$ at $x = 3$.
$\text{(A)} \ 19683 \qquad  \text{(B)} \ 531441 \qquad  \text{(C)} \ 14348907 \qquad  \text{(D)} \ 387420489 \qquad  \text{(E)} \ 27^{27}$

Mary is $25\%$ older than Sally, and Sally is $45\%$ younger than Danielle. The sum of their ages is $25.5$ years. How old will Mary be on her next birthday?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 7\qquad \mathrm{(B)}\ 8\qquad \mathrm{(C)}\ 9\qquad \mathrm{(D)}\ 10\qquad \mathrm{(E)}\ 11$

How many 4-digit positive integers have four different digits, where the leading digit is not zero, the integer is a multiple of 4, and 6 is the largest digit?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 48 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 50 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 56 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 60 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 65$

The bar graph illustrates the results of a survey on color preferences. What percent preferred orange?
$\text{(A)}\ 12\% \qquad \text{(B)}\ 15\% \qquad \text{(C)}\ 18\% \qquad \text{(D)}\ 20\% \qquad \text{(E)}\ 23\%$

If $x = 2 + 2^p$ and $y = 1 + 2^{-p}$, then express $y$ in terms of $x$.
A) $\frac{x-1}{x-2}$
B) $\frac{x-1}{x+2}$
C) $\frac{x+1}{x-2}$
D) $\frac{x+3}{x-2}$

Kaleana, Quay, Marty, and Shana are comparing their test scores. Kaleana reveals her score is 75, but the others keep theirs hidden. Quay thinks, "At least two of us have the same score." Marty thinks, "I didn't get the highest score." Shana thinks, "I didn't get the lowest score." Assuming that all scores are different from each other except for the cases explicitly known, list the scores from lowest to highest for Marty (M), Quay (Q), and Shana (S).
(A) S, Q, M
(B) Q, S, M
(C) Q, M, S
(D) M, Q, S
(E) S, M, Q

In triangle $\triangle ABC$, point D is placed on side AC such that ∠ADB is straight. If ∠BAD = y', ∠ADC = z', and ∠BDC = w', determine the measure of ∠ABC in terms of y', z', and w'.
- **(A)** $180^\circ + w' - z'$
- **(B)** $180^\circ - y' + z'$
- **(C)** $180^\circ - z' + y'$
- **(D)** $180^\circ - z' - y'$

What is the value of $\dfrac{13! - 12!}{10!}$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 1548\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 1584\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 1652\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 1698$

From a regular decagon, a triangle is formed by connecting three randomly chosen vertices of the decagon. What is the probability that at least one of the sides of the triangle is also a side of the decagon?
$\textbf{(A) } \frac{1}{2} \qquad \textbf{(B) } \frac{2}{3} \qquad \textbf{(C) } \frac{7}{12} \qquad \textbf{(D) } \frac{3}{4} \qquad \textbf{(E) } \frac{5}{6}$

Determine the pairs of values of $x$ and $y$ that are the common solutions of the equations $y = (x+2)^2$ and $xy + y = 2$.
A) $(2^{1/3} - 2, 2^{2/3})$
B) $(0, 4)$
C) $(2, 16)$
D) No solutions

Suppose you have $30$ quarters and $20$ dimes. If these are equivalent in value to $15$ quarters and $m$ dimes, what does $m=$?
A) 55
B) 57  
C) 58  
D) 60

The perimeter of a regular pentagon exceeds the perimeter of a square by $2023 \ \text{cm}$. The length of each side of the pentagon exceeds the length of each side of the square by $d \ \text{cm}$. The square has a perimeter greater than 0. How many positive integers are NOT possible values for $d$?
$\text{(A)} \ 0$
$\text{(B)} \ 9$
$\text{(C)} \ 221$
$\text{(D)} \ 663$
$\text{(E)} \ \infty$

A laser is placed at the point $(4,6)$. The laser beam travels in a straight line. Larry wants the beam to hit and bounce off the $y$-axis, then hit and bounce off the $x$-axis twice, before hitting the point $(8,6)$. What is the total distance the beam will travel along this path?
$\textbf{(A) }10\sqrt{2} \qquad \textbf{(B) }12\sqrt{2} \qquad \textbf{(C) }14\sqrt{2} \qquad \textbf{(D) }16\sqrt{2} \qquad \textbf{(E) }18\sqrt{2}$

Triangle $PQR$ is inscribed in a circle. The measures of the non-overlapping minor arcs $PQ$, $QR$, and $RP$ are, respectively, $x + 80^\circ$, $3x - 30^\circ$, and $2x + 10^\circ$. Find one of the interior angles of the triangle.
A) $55^\circ$
B) $60^\circ$
C) $65^\circ$
D) $70^\circ$

Given a right triangle with hypotenuse $a+b$, calculate the area of a new triangle that has three times the area of the original triangle.
**A)** $\frac{1}{2}ab$  
**B)** $2ab$  
**C)** $\frac{3}{2}ab$  
**D)** $ab$  
**E)** $\frac{3}{2}(a+b)^2$

Trapezoid $PQRS$ has $PQ||RS$, $QS = 2$, $\angle QSP = 30^{\circ}$, and $\angle SRP = 60^{\circ}$. The ratio $RS: PQ$ is $7: 3$. What is $PR$?
A) $\frac{1}{3}$
B) $\frac{5}{3}$
C) $\frac{6}{3}$
D) $\frac{8}{3}$
E) $\frac{10}{3}$

a: The final answer given the choices is (E) 500.

p:
In a trapezoid, the length of the line segment joining the midpoints of the diagonals is $5$. If the longer base has a length of $85$, determine the length of the shorter base.
A) 70
B) 72
C) 74
D) 75
E) 77

How many four-digit integers have a remainder of 1 when divided by 7, a remainder of 3 when divided by 10, and a remainder of 5 when divided by 13?
$\textbf{(A) }3\qquad\textbf{(B) }4\qquad\textbf{(C) }5\qquad\textbf{(D) }6\qquad\textbf{(E) }7$

Tom, Dick, and Harry started out on a 120-mile journey. Tom and Dick went by automobile at the rate of 30 mph, while Harry cycled at the rate of 15 mph. After traveling a certain distance, Tom got off and walked on at 5 mph, while Dick went back for Harry and brought him to the destination at the same time that Tom arrived. How many hours were required for the trip?
**A.** 7 hours
**B.** 8 hours
**C.** 9 hours
**D.** 10 hours
**E.** 11 hours

A rectangular photograph measures $12$ inches in height and $16$ inches in width. It is placed in a frame with a $3$ inches wide border on all sides. Additionally, there is an inner decorative lining of $1$ inch width between the photograph and the frame. What is the area of the border, including the decorative lining, in square inches?
$\textbf{(A)}\hspace{.05in}280\qquad\textbf{(B)}\hspace{.05in}288\qquad\textbf{(C)}\hspace{.05in}296\qquad\textbf{(D)}\hspace{.05in}304\qquad\textbf{(E)}\hspace{.05in}312$

A farmer's rectangular field is divided into a 2 by 2 grid of 4 rectangular sections. The farmer plans to plant five different crops: corn, wheat, soybeans, potatoes, or oats. The farmer does not want to grow corn adjacent to wheat, soybeans adjacent to potatoes, and oats should not be adjacent to corn. In how many distinct ways can the farmer plant these crops in the sections of the field under these constraints?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 72 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 90 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 157 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 180 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 215$

Evaluate the expression $3 - (-3)^{3-(-3)} \times 2$.
A) -729
B) -1458
C) 729
D) -1455

The Fibonacci sequence $1,1,2,3,5,8,13,21,\ldots$ starts with two 1s, and each term afterwards is the sum of its two predecessors. Which one of the twelve digits is the last to appear in the units position of a number in the Fibonacci sequence, when considered modulo 12?
$\textbf{(A)} \ 0  \qquad \textbf{(B)} \ 2  \qquad \textbf{(C)} \ 10  \qquad \textbf{(D)} \ 11$
$\textbf{(A)} \ 0  \qquad \textbf{(B)} \ 2  \qquad \textbf{(C)} \ 10  \qquad \textbf{(D)} \ 11$

Given the table below, find the correct formula relating x and y:
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline x & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5\\ \hline y & 3 & 11 & 23 & 39 & 59 \\ \hline\end{array}$$
Which of the following correctly represents the relationship between x and y?
**A.** $y = x^2 + x + 1$
**B.** $y = 2x^2 + 2x - 1$
**C.** $y = x^3 + 1$
**D.** $y = x^2 + 3x + 1$
**E.** None of these

The ratio of the areas of two concentric circles is $1:4$. If the area of the smaller circle is $4\pi$ square units, find the difference between the areas of the two circles.
- **(A)** $8\pi$ square units
- **(B)** $10\pi$ square units
- **(C)** $12\pi$ square units
- **(D)** $14\pi$ square units
- **(E)** $16\pi$ square units

A triangular array of $3003$ coins has $1$ coin in the first row, $2$ coins in the second row, $3$ coins in the third row, and so on up to $N$ coins in the $N$th row. What is the sum of the digits of $N$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 12\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 13\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 14\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 15\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 16$

A spider moves along a number line, starting at $3$. It moves to $-4$, then turns around and moves to $8$. Determine the total distance the spider moves.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 16\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 18\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 19\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 21\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 23$

Consider a $4 \times 4$ grid where each cell contains a unique integer from $1$ to $16$. The grid must be filled so that the entries in every row, every column, and both main diagonals (from top-left to bottom-right and from top-right to bottom-left) are in strictly increasing order. How many such grids are possible?
**A)** 8
**B)** 16
**C)** 24
**D)** 32

Alice needs to replace a light bulb located $15$ centimeters below the ceiling in her living room. The ceiling is $2.8$ meters above the floor. Alice is $1.5$ meters tall and can reach up to $40$ centimeters above the top of her head when fully extending her arms. However, due to a recent injury, she can only reach $30$ centimeters above her head. Standing on a stool, she can just barely touch the light bulb. What is the minimum height of the stool, in centimeters, that Alice needs?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 75 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 85\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 95\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 105\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 115$

Find the pairs of values of $x$ and $y$ that satisfy the equations $y = (x+1)^4$ and $xy + y = \cos(\pi x)$.
A) $(0,1)$  
B) $(1,32)$  
C) $(-1,0)$  
D) No solution

In an isosceles triangle, two angles are $50^\circ$ and $x^\circ$. Calculate the sum of all possible values of $x$.
A) 145
B) 165
C) 180
D) 195
E) 210

If $4x^3 - 16x^2 + mx - 20$ is divisible by $x-4$, find the value of $m$ for which it is also divisible by $x-5$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ m = 4\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ m = 5\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ m = 6\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ m = 8 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ m = 10$

Determine the times between 7 and 8 o'clock, correct to the nearest minute, when the hands of a clock will form an angle of $120^\circ$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 7:15 \text{ and } 7:30\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 7:16 \text{ and } 7:27\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 7:17 \text{ and } 7:26\\ \textbf{(D)}\ 7:18 \text{ and } 7:25\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 7:14 \text{ and } 7:28$

Each of the $6$ sides and the $9$ diagonals of a regular hexagon are randomly and independently colored red, blue, or green with equal probability. What is the probability that there will be a triangle whose vertices are among the vertices of the hexagon such that all of its sides have the same color?
A) $\frac{3}{4}$
B) $\frac{880}{1000}$
C) $\frac{872}{1000}$
D) $\frac{850}{1000}$

Shauna takes five tests, each worth a maximum of $120$ points. Her scores on the first three tests are $86$, $112$, and $91$. In order to average $95$ for all five tests, what is the lowest score she could earn on one of the other two tests?
$\textbf{(A) }60\qquad\textbf{(B) }66\qquad\textbf{(C) }72\qquad\textbf{(D) }78\qquad\textbf{(E) }84$

Define $x \heartsuit y = x^2 - y^2$ for all real numbers $x$ and $y$. Which of the following statements is true?
(A) $x \heartsuit y = y \heartsuit x$ for all $x$ and $y$
(B) $(x+y) \heartsuit (x-y) = 2x^2 - 2y^2$ for all $x$ and $y$
(C) $x \heartsuit 0 = x^2$ for all $x$
(D) $x \heartsuit x = 0$ for all $x$
(E) $x \heartsuit y > 0$ if $x > y$
(A) $x \heartsuit y = y \heartsuit x$ for all $x$ and $y$
(B) $(x+y) \heartsuit (x-y) = 2x^2 - 2y^2$ for all $x$ and $y$
(C) $x \heartsuit 0 = x^2$ for all $x$
(D) $x \heartsuit x = 0$ for all $x$
(E) $x \heartsuit y > 0$ if $x > y$

Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen divisor of $15!$ is divisible by $3$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{7} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ \frac{2}{7} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \frac{4}{7} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \frac{5}{7} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \frac{6}{7}$

In a 4x4 version of tic-tac-toe, Azar places an 'X' in one of the boxes, and Carl places an 'O' in one of the remaining boxes. This continues alternately. The game ends when one player places their third symbol in a row (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal), or the board is filled. Suppose the players make their moves at random and Carl wins the game by placing his third 'O'. How many ways can the board look after the game is over?
A) 11400
B) 114400
C) 100000
D) 120000

Each piece of candy in a store costs a whole number of cents. Casper has exactly enough money to buy either $10$ pieces of red candy, $18$ pieces of green candy, $20$ pieces of blue candy, or $n$ pieces of yellow candy. A piece of yellow candy costs $30$ cents. What is the smallest possible value of $n$?
$\textbf{(A) } 6 \qquad \textbf{(B) } 8 \qquad \textbf{(C) } 10\qquad \textbf{(D) } 12 \qquad \textbf{(E) } 14$

At the beginning of the semester, in Mr. Brown's class, $40\%$ of students answered "Yes" to the question "Do you enjoy history", $40\%$ answered "No," and the remaining $20\%$ were "Undecided." By the end of the semester, $60\%$ answered "Yes", $30\%$ answered "No", and $10\%$ remained "Undecided." What is the difference between the maximum and minimum possible values of $y$% of students who changed their answer?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 30\% \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 40\% \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 50\% \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 60\% \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 70\%$

The keystone arch is composed of 8 congruent isosceles trapezoids fitted together along their non-parallel sides. The bottom sides of the two end trapezoids are horizontal. In this arch, let $y$ be the angle measure in degrees of the smaller interior angle of the trapezoid. Determine the value of $y$.
A) 72.5°  
B) 76.25°  
C) 78.75°  
D) 81.25°  
E) 85°

What is the value of $\left[(2^2 - 1 + 3^3)^{-1} - 4\right] \div 2?$
$\textbf{(A)}\ -\frac{125}{60}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ -\frac{119}{60}\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{60}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ -\frac{121}{60}$

A tetrahedral pyramid has an equilateral triangular base with side length 3 and has lateral faces that are equilateral triangles. A cube is placed within the tetrahedral pyramid such that one vertex of the cube touches the centroid of the triangular base and its opposite edges touch the midpoints of the slant edges of the pyramid. What is the volume of this cube?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 1$
$\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$
$\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
$\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{2}$
$\textbf{(E)}\ 0.5$

If you walk for 60 minutes at a rate of 3 mph and then run for 45 minutes at a rate of 8 mph, how many miles will you have traveled at the end of one hour and 45 minutes?
$\text{(A)}\ 7\text{ miles} \quad \text{(B)}\ 8\text{ miles} \quad \text{(C)}\ 9\text{ miles} \quad \text{(D)}\ 10\text{ miles} \quad \text{(E)}\ 12\text{ miles}$

Forty-one books are arranged from left to right in order of increasing prices. The price of each book differs by $3 from that of each adjacent book. The sum of the prices of the first and the last books equals the total price of the two middle books. Then:
- What is the price of the first book if the sum of the first and last books is $246?
A) $63
B) $60
C) $57
D) $54
E) $51

Consider the data set $[7, 20, 34, 34, 40, 42, 42, 44, 52, 58]$. The median $Q_2$ is $41$, the first quartile $Q_1$ is $34$, and the third quartile $Q_3$ is $44$. An outlier is defined as a value that is more than 1.5 times the interquartile range below the first quartile or above the third quartile. Determine the number of outliers in this data set.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 4$

The sum of two natural numbers is $23,405$. One of the two numbers is divisible by $5$. If the units digit of that number is erased, the resulting number is five times the other number. What is the difference between these two numbers?
$\textbf{(A)} ~19,500\qquad\textbf{(B)} ~20,550\qquad\textbf{(C)} ~21,600\qquad\textbf{(D)} ~22,650\qquad\textbf{(E)} ~23,700$

Factor the quadratic expression $15x^2 + bx + 15$ into two linear binomial factors with integer coefficients. This will be possible if $b$ is:
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{any odd number} \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \text{some odd number} \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \text{any even number}$
$\textbf{(D)}\ \text{some even number} \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \text{zero}$

$\textbf{(D)}\ \text{some even number} \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \text{zero}$

- (A) 25
- (B) 50
- (C) 70
- (D) 105
- (E) 125

What is the probability that a randomly drawn positive factor of $90$ is less than $10$?
A) $\frac{1}{12}$
B) $\frac{1}{6}$
C) $\frac{1}{3}$
D) $\frac{1}{4}$
E) $\frac{1}{2}$

Tess decides to modify her running path around a triangular block formed by points $J$, $K$, and $M$ directly connected. She starts running counterclockwise from corner $J$. Which graph could represent her straight-line distance from home as she runs around this new triangular block?
A) Graph peaking at $K$, leveling and descending
B) Graph increases continuously without droppings
C) Graph starts at a high point and only drops
D) Graph is a continuous loop with cyclic peaks
E) Graph first dips down, then rises, and finally decreases
ch: Graph peaking at $K$, leveling and descending
Graph starts at a high point and only drops
Graph is a continuous loop with cyclic peaks
Graph first dips down, then rises, and finally decreases

The perimeter of a rectangle exceeds the perimeter of an equilateral triangle by 1950 cm. The length of each side of the rectangle exceeds the length of each side of the triangle by $ d \ \text{cm} $. All sides of the triangle are equal, and the rectangle is such that its length is triple that of its width. The triangle has a perimeter greater than 0. Determine how many positive integers are NOT possible values for $ d $.
- (A) 0
- (B) 195
- (C) 650
- (D) 780
- (E) 1950

Li reads a new novel called "Numbers in Nature". On the first day, Li reads $1/4$ of the pages plus $10$ more, and on the second day he reads $1/3$ of the remaining pages plus $20$ pages. On the third day he reads $1/2$ of the remaining pages plus $40$ pages. Finally, he notices there are $50$ pages left, which he finishes the next day. How many pages are in this novel?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 340 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 360\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 420\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 460\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 500$

The numerator of a fraction is $5x + 2$, and the denominator is $8 - 3x$. Find the values of $x$ for which the numerator is greater than the denominator, given that $x$ can have any value between $-1$ and $3$, both included.
**A)** $0 \leq x \leq 3$  
**B)** $\frac{3}{4} \leq x \leq 3$  
**C)** $\frac{3}{4} < x \leq 3$  
**D)** $-1 \leq x \leq 3$  
**E)** $\frac{3}{4} < x < 3$

Alice uses a sequence writing method, starting with the first term as 7. To generate the next term, she flips a fair coin. If it results in heads, she triples the previous term and subtracts 2. If tails, she takes a third of the previous term and subtracts 2. Determine the probability that the fourth term in Alice's sequence is an integer.
$\mathrm{(A)}\ \frac{1}{4}\qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \frac{1}{3}\qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \frac{1}{2}\qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \frac{3}{4}\qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ \frac{5}{6}$

We want to find three-digit numbers where the middle digit $B$ equals twice the last digit $C$ minus the first digit $A$. The condition given is:
$$ B = 2C - A $$
Additionally, $A$ must be even.

#### Steps for
A. 18
B. 19
C. 20
D. 21
E. 22

A paper triangle with sides of lengths $6, 8,$ and $10$ inches is folded so that point $B$ falls on point $C$. Determine the length in inches of the crease.
A) $\frac{10}{3}$
B) $\frac{16}{3}$
C) $6$
D) $\frac{20}{3}$

For each real number $a$ with $0 \leq a \leq 1$, let the number $x$ be chosen randomly from the interval $[0, a]$ and the number $y$ be chose from the interval $[0, 1-x]$. Calculate the probability $Q(a)$ that:
$$\sin^2{(\pi x)} + \sin^2{(\pi y)} > \frac{1}{2}$$
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{7}{12} \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 2 - \sqrt{2} \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{4} \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{5}{8}$

Consider the quadratic equation $4x^2 - 2x\sqrt{2} + 3 = 0$. Find the nature of its roots.
A) Real and Equal  
B) Real and Distinct  
C) Complex and Non-real  
D) Imaginary

A rectangle EFGH has EF = 5 and EH = 6. Point H is the center of a circle, and point F is on the circle. Calculate the exact area of the shaded region formed by the quarter circle that includes point F and the area of rectangle EFGH.
A) $\frac{61\pi}{4} - 25$
B) $\frac{61\pi}{4} - 30$
C) $\frac{61\pi}{4} - 35$
D) $\frac{61\pi}{4} - 40$

Define $\boxed{n}$ to be the sum of the positive factors of $n$.
For example, $\boxed{6} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 6 = 12$. Find $\boxed{\boxed{15}}$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 36 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 48 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 54 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 60 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 72$

Paul owes Paula $45$ cents and has a pocket full of $5$-cent coins, $10$-cent coins, $20$-cent coins, and $25$-cent coins that he can use to pay her. What is the difference between the largest and the smallest number of coins he can use to pay her?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 4 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 5 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 6 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 7 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 8$

A basket contains $600$ balls, of which $70\%$ are green and the remaining are yellow. How many green balls must be removed so that $60\%$ of the remaining balls are green?
A) 120
B) 130
C) 140
D) 150
E) 160

In a certain year, the price of gasoline rose by $30\%$ during January, fell by $10\%$ and then rose by $15\%$ during February, rose by $15\%$ during March, and fell by $x\%$ during April. The price of gasoline at the end of April was the same as it had been at the beginning of January. To the nearest integer, what is $x$?
A) 25
B) 30
C) 35
D) 40
E) 45

Six positive consecutive integers starting with $c$ have an average of $d$. What is the average of $6$ consecutive integers that start with $d$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ c+4\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ c+4.5\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ c+5\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ c+5.5\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ c+6$

At Dave's Diner, a burger costs $4 each and fries cost $3 each. If there is a promotional offer that gives a $2 discount on each burger when buying at least 4 burgers, how much will it cost to purchase 6 burgers and 5 fries?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 25\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 27\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 29\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 30\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 32$

How many whole numbers between $200$ and $500$ include the digit $3$?
$\text{(A)}\ 130 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 138 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 140 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 150 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 160$

Let rectangle $ABCD$ have length $2$ and width $1$. Attached to rectangle $ABCD$ are two similar rectangles $EFGH$ and $IJKL$ along sides $AB$ and $CD$ respectively, such that the ratio of the sides of $EFGH$ to $ABCD$ is $1:2$. If $EC = x$, find $EC$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{4}$
$\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{1}{3}$
$\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$
$\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{2}$
$\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

Millie stocks a bird feeder with 2 liters of seeds on Monday, 30% of which are millet. Each successive day, she adds another 2 liters of seeds, reducing the proportion of other seeds by 10% each day, while keeping the millet content constant at 30%. Each day the birds consume 30% of the millet and all of the other seeds left from the previous day. Determine on which day, just after Millie has added the seeds, the birds will find more than half of the seeds in the feeder are millet.
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{Tuesday}\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ \text{Wednesday}\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \text{Thursday}\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \text{Friday}\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \text{Saturday}$

$ABCD$ is a rectangle with $M$ as the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$. $\overline{MS} \perp \overline{BD}$, and $\overline{MR} \perp \overline{AC}$. $\overline{CF} \perp \overline{BD}$, and $\overline{MQ} \perp \overline{CF}$. Then $MR + MS$ is equal to:

**C)** $ 4 - 2\sqrt{3} $  
**D)** $ 4 + 2\sqrt{3} $

Determine the number of distinct real solutions $ x $ that satisfy the equation $$ (3x^2 - 8)^2 = 25 $$.
$\textbf{(A) }2\qquad\textbf{(B) }3\qquad\textbf{(C) }4\qquad\textbf{(D) }6\qquad\textbf{(E) }8$

In circle $\Omega$, triangle $PQR$ is inscribed. The non-overlapping minor arcs $PQ$, $QR$, and $RP$ have measures $2x+60^\circ$, $3x-15^\circ$, and $4x+10^\circ$ respectively. If the tangent to the circle at point $Q$ forms an angle of $30^\circ$ with line $PQ$, find the measure of angle $P$ in the triangle.
A) $40.33^\circ$
B) $43.33^\circ$
C) $45.33^\circ$
D) $48.33^\circ$

Determine the values of integers $a$, $b$, and $c$ satisfying the equation
$$a(a-b)+b(b-c)+c(c-a) = 2.$$
What condition among the following is sufficient to guarantee that the equation holds true?
$\textbf{(A)} \: a=b$ and $b=c+1$
$\textbf{(B)} \: a=b+1$ and $b=c+1$
$\textbf{(C)} \: a=c+2$ and $b=a+1$
$\textbf{(D)} \: a=c$ and $b-1=c$
$\textbf{(E)} \: a+b+c=2$

$\textbf{(B)} \: a=b+1$ and $b=c+1$

: a+b+c=2$
